---
description: "A note on process. This is live, in-progress work written in the open, with AI in the loop and the judgment mine."
toc: false
---

# How This Book Is Being Written {.unnumbered}

A note on process, since you are here and it matters. This book is written in the
open, which means it is a live experiment as much as it is a text. It is my
sabbatical project, and from July 2026 through the first half of 2027 my main work
is to build it out.

The question I am chasing is how AI can genuinely help us do computer
architecture. It is easy to have opinions about AI from the sidelines. I think it
is harder, and more useful, to work with these systems every day and feel where
they help and where they fail. If I am going to argue that engineering is changing
because of AI, I should experience that change firsthand rather than theorize
about it from a distance. And this is not only about writing. Alongside the book I
am building code that I hope to release with it. All of it is skunkworks, out in
the open.

Let me be candid. Because I am leaning hard on generative AI, and experimenting
with agentic workflows and other creative uses of these tools, some of what you
find here will be rough, and some of it will read like AI slop. Catching that and
cutting it is part of the work. But the ideas, arguments, structure, and direction
are mine. I spend most of my time thinking through the questions I want to answer,
shaping the narrative, and deciding what the contribution is. I then use a workflow
of several AI agents to brainstorm alternatives, challenge my assumptions,
reorganize material, critique drafts, and help me refine the writing. What I am
still learning is how to govern that process, knowing when to accept a suggestion,
when to reject it outright, and when fluent language is quietly hiding a weak idea.
That judgment is mine to develop, and I will not develop it by avoiding these
tools.

From my experience so far, and I was at this part time for more than six months
before opening it up, the work does not move in a straight line. It goes two steps
forward and one step back. When things seem to be going well, I get confident and
let the engine run, and that is often exactly when it inverts and I have to walk it
back. Some weeks it is one step forward and three steps back. That has happened
more times than I can count.

This is also new ground, and part of why I am writing it is that I have not fully
figured it out. I have been circling these ideas for about three years, since an
early IEEE article that set out the foundations, and I still have not landed them
cleanly. There is no finished resource to go read, so I am building this area with
my research group, writing it in pieces and trying to sift a coherent picture out
of them. The surest way I know to understand something is to teach it. This book is
my attempt to teach a subject I am still learning.

All of that to say, some of this will be rough, and much of it will change. Take
what you see with a grain of salt until I sign off on it, which is still a few
months out. So why put it out now, rough as it is? Because I would rather paint
with the studio door open. People who work in public open themselves to criticism,
and they tend to get sharper because of it, not despite it. I have always believed
in building this way, and I do not see publishing as the end of the process. I see
it as the start of a conversation. If a passage is vague, imprecise, or wrong, or
if you just want to talk it through, please bear with me and feel free to
[reach out](https://vijay.seas.harvard.edu).

Vijay Janapa Reddi
